items.summary: The 2026-05-28 23:14 UTC interactive sign-in to corp.com by ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com from a residential-proxy IP, and what it represents.
items.correct_label: Part of the real intrusion — initial access to the tenant via a stale, never-deprovisioned external-auditor account whose credentials were compromised off-platform.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The account's provenance/IAM record — provisioned for an audit engagement that closed in December 2025, never deprovisioned, retains an MFA conditional-access exclusion, and had no interactive sign-ins between then and 2026-05-28. The Low Identity Protection flag and the unfamiliar source IP alone are NOT sufficient: they are indistinguishable from roughly 40 similar low/medium-risk sign-ins across the tenant that week.

items.summary: The OAuth app 'Mailbox Archiver Pro', registered and granted Mail.Read/Files.Read.All by ext-auditor-rwilcox within 20 minutes of the anomalous sign-in, with a service principal created for it.
items.correct_label: Part of the real intrusion — the compromised account used its session to register a malicious OAuth app and grant it mail/file read scopes, establishing a persistence mechanism independent of the account itself.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The correlated Entra ID audit trail showing app registration, delegated user-consent grant (Mail.Read, Files.Read.All), and service-principal creation — all by ext-auditor-rwilcox within a ~60-second window minutes after the anomalous sign-in, with no corresponding change ticket. A bare listing of the app registration alone (unverified publisher, recently registered) is NOT sufficient on its own — several benign apps in this tenant also have mail/file scopes, and one other benign app was also registered recently by an unverified publisher.

items.summary: The 'Mailbox Archiver Pro' service principal's recurring access (2026-05-29 onward) to the CEO, CFO, and M&A-lead mailboxes and the 'Project Atlas' SharePoint site.
items.correct_label: Part of the real intrusion — the malicious app's service principal has been reading the CEO/CFO/M&A-lead mailboxes and the Project Atlas (active M&A workstream) SharePoint site roughly daily for about two weeks, and as of the current date this access remains live and unremediated.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The service-principal sign-in history together with its Microsoft Graph activity pattern (recurring reads against the three named mailboxes) AND the SharePoint Online access audit for 'Project Atlas' (which independently corroborates the same service-principal identity reading M&A files on the same cadence). Noting only that the app 'has mail/file scopes' is NOT sufficient — several benign apps also have broad mail/file scopes and high access volume; what's decisive is which specific resources this service principal is shown reading.

items.summary: MailItemsAccessed (Exchange Online mailbox-audit) records are unavailable for 2026-05-29 through 2026-05-31, the first three days of the malicious app's mailbox access.
items.correct_label: A genuine, pre-existing logging-coverage gap (MailItemsAccessed was enabled tenant-wide only from 2026-06-01) — not evidence of tampering or log deletion. The exact scope of the early-window mailbox reads must be inferred from the service principal's sign-ins, Graph activity pattern, and the SharePoint access audit (which has no such gap), rather than read directly from mailbox-audit records.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The explicit note that mailbox-audit (MailItemsAccessed) logging was not yet enabled tenant-wide for the 2026-05-29 to 05-31 window, combined with the Hunter inferring the early-window access scope from the service principal's sign-ins/Graph pattern and the SharePoint access audit instead. Treating the missing records themselves as suspicious, or as a sign the attacker disabled/cleared logs, would be an error — there is no evidence anywhere in this environment of log tampering.

items.summary: OAuth app 'CorpVault Backup' — long-standing, IT-registered, verified-publisher integration with Mail.Read + Files.Read scopes and nightly tenant-wide service-principal activity.
items.correct_label: Benign — a long-standing, IT-registered, verified-publisher, ticketed integration (CHG-2024-0312) with two years of consistent nightly activity; not part of the intrusion.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The app's registration details (registered 2024 by IT, verified publisher, change ticket CHG-2024-0312) and its long, consistent history of nightly activity. Its broad mail/file scopes and high access volume alone are NOT decisive — that profile is normal for several apps in this tenant; what distinguishes it is registrant, publisher verification, ticket, and history.

items.summary: OAuth app 'Onna eDiscovery' — IT-registered, verified-publisher legal-hold/eDiscovery tool that accesses the CEO and M&A-lead mailboxes under an active legal hold (LH-2026-014).
items.correct_label: Benign — a long-standing, IT-registered, verified-publisher, ticketed (CHG-2023-0188) legal-hold tool; its access to the CEO and M&A-lead mailboxes is explained by an active, documented legal hold and is not part of the intrusion.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The app's registration details (registered 2023 by IT, verified publisher, change ticket CHG-2023-0188) and the active legal hold (LH-2026-014) that explains its access to two of the same mailboxes the malicious app also reads. The mere fact that it accesses the CEO's and M&A-lead's mailboxes is NOT by itself suspicious or evidence against this app — that overlap is coincidental and explained by the legal hold, not evidence of coordination with the malicious app.

items.summary: OAuth app 'HubSpot Connector' — IT-registered, verified-publisher Sales CRM integration scoped to the Sales team's mailboxes only.
items.correct_label: Benign — a long-standing, IT-registered, verified-publisher, ticketed (CHG-2022-0405) integration scoped only to Sales mailboxes; not part of the intrusion.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The app's registration details (registered 2022 by IT, verified publisher, change ticket CHG-2022-0405) and its scope being limited to Sales-team mailboxes, with no access to executive or M&A resources.

items.summary: OAuth app 'PowerBI-Finance' — IT-registered, Microsoft-verified-publisher integration with Files.Read.All via a service account, scheduled refreshes against the Finance Reporting SharePoint site only.
items.correct_label: Benign — a long-standing, IT-registered, Microsoft-verified-publisher, ticketed (CHG-2023-0091) integration, service-account driven, scoped to a single non-M&A SharePoint site; not part of the intrusion.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The app's registration details (registered 2023 by IT, Microsoft-verified publisher, change ticket CHG-2023-0091, driven by service account svc_powerbi) and its access being limited to the 'Finance Reporting' SharePoint site, with no mailbox access and no access to 'Project Atlas'.

items.summary: OAuth app 'Expense Insights' — registered 2026-06-02, unverified publisher, Mail.Read scoped only to the shared receipts@corp.com mailbox.
items.correct_label: Benign — a recently-registered, unverified-publisher app with mail scopes, but IT-registered under a change ticket (CHG-2026-0608), admin-consented, and scoped to a single non-executive shared mailbox; not part of the intrusion. Recency of registration and an unverified publisher are not by themselves indicators of compromise.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The app's registration details (registered 2026-06-02 by IT, change ticket CHG-2026-0608, admin consent by jdoe) and its access being limited to the shared receipts@corp.com mailbox. Citing only 'recently registered' and/or 'unverified publisher' as suspicious — without checking the registrant, ticket, and scope — would be an error, since those two traits alone are shared with the malicious app and do not distinguish this one from it.

items.summary: 2026-06-08 to 06-10 activity by pentest-svc@corp.com / app 'RedTeam-Recon-Tool': impossible-travel sign-ins, bulk directory enumeration via Directory.Read.All/User.Read.All, and at least 5 unactioned HIGH Identity Protection alerts.
items.correct_label: Authorized — a pre-approved penetration test (engagement PT-2026-03, completed 2026-06-10); not part of the real intrusion, despite presenting far more obvious attack indicators than the actual compromise.
items.decisive_evidence_description: The PT-2026-03 engagement record in the GRC/ITSM system of record (vendor, authorized window, scope, CISO approval, pre-registered account/app). The account name (pentest-svc), the app name (RedTeam-Recon-Tool), the recon-only nature of its activity, or the absence of any follow-on access to mailboxes/SharePoint are NOT sufficient grounds for an authorization verdict on their own — a real attacker could present the same surface profile, and the only way to confirm authorization is the engagement record.

items.summary: 2026-05-26 password-spray burst from 45.142.0.0/24 against ~80 accounts including ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com, generating 5 unactioned HIGH Identity Protection alerts and 6 smart-lockouts.
items.correct_label: Unrelated noise — a failed password-spray burst with zero successful authentications from the source range; not the vector used to compromise ext-auditor-rwilcox, whose actual compromise three days later came from a different IP entirely.
items.decisive_evidence_description: Explicit confirmation that zero sign-ins from 45.142.0.0/24 ever succeeded, against any account, at any time. The alert volume, lockout count, or the fact that ext-auditor-rwilcox was among the targeted accounts are NOT sufficient on their own to either implicate or rule out this burst — only the zero-successful-authentications check does.
